discovery.text: From Allan B. Jacobs' Great Streets (1993). The great Prussian boulevard — lined with linden trees as the name promises, connecting the Stadtschloss to the Brandenburg Gate.

narrative.summary: The great Prussian boulevard — lined with linden trees as the name promises, connecting the Stadtschloss to the Brandenburg Gate.

blocks.title: Why Jacobs Chose It
blocks.body: Wide central promenade, grand institutional buildings (Humboldt University, the Arsenal, the opera), linden trees in double rows, the Brandenburger Tor anchoring the west end. One of the few great streets that survived the 20th century more or less intact.

blocks.title: How to Walk It
blocks.body: Walk the full length. Jacobs is explicit: you have to walk a great street to understand it — the sequence, the proportions, how the edges change. Give it an hour minimum. Sit at a café or on a bench midway. Notice what makes it feel alive.

blocks.name: EINSTEIN Unter den Linden
blocks.why: The classic Viennese-style café right on Unter den Linden. Coffee, newspapers, people-watching under the lindens — the street's slow civic ritual. Exactly where Jacobs' 'sitting and staying' happens.

blocks.name: German Historical Museum
blocks.why: The Zeughaus — Berlin's oldest surviving baroque building, directly on the boulevard at the bridge to Museumsinsel. The great cultural institution that gives Unter den Linden its civic weight and defines its eastern terminus.

blocks.name: Dussmann das KulturKaufhaus
blocks.why: One block off Unter den Linden on Friedrichstraße — Berlin's greatest cultural department store: books, music, films, open until midnight. The edge institution that rewards those who step off the main boulevard.

blocks.name: Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin
blocks.why: The Prussian State Library, directly on the boulevard. An extraordinary Wilhelmine building that anchors the institutional weight of the western end. Reading rooms open to the public — the contemplative counterpoint to street life.
